name: Jimmy Dorsey
description: American clarinetist, alto saxophonist, bandleader, and composer, brother of Tommy Dorsey (1904–1957)
abstract: James Francis Dorsey (February 29, 1904 – June 12, 1957) was an American jazz clarinetist, saxophonist, composer and big band leader. He recorded and composed the jazz and pop standards "I'm Glad There Is You (In This World of Ordinary People)" and "It's the Dreamer in Me".

about_quotes.speaker: Dan D'Imperio
about_quotes.evidence: in 1956 Tommy and Jimmy Dorsey, the Dorsey Brothers Orchestra, came to Cortland... I walked in there... the band was ferocious man

about_quotes.speaker: Herb Ellis
about_quotes.evidence: I had been with Jimmy Dorsey... Jimmy gave me a couple of solos here and there... he says, 'I want you to write for Herb.'
about_quotes.doc_title: Herb Ellis interviewed by Michael Woods, Los Angeles, California, September 3, 1995 [interview 1 of 2]

about_quotes.speaker: Nick Brignola
about_quotes.evidence: most of the band leaders back then played clarinet. It was Benny Goodman, Artie Shaw, Jimmy Dorsey, Woody Herman.
about_quotes.doc_title: Nick Brignola interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, July 28, 1997

about_quotes.speaker: Keith Ingham
about_quotes.evidence: I believe he [Charlie Parker] was quoted as saying he loved Jimmy Dorsey’s alto saxophone playing
about_quotes.doc_title: Keith Ingham interviewed by Monk Rowe, Chautauqua, New York, September 12, 1997

about_quotes.speaker: Sam Jones
about_quotes.evidence: they wanted to write about Red Nichols and some of them old, Jimmy Dorsey and people like that.

about_quotes.speaker: Mandel Johnny
about_quotes.evidence: I went with Jimmy Dorsey, playing lead trombone in the toughest book in the country
about_quotes.doc_title: Mandel Johnny — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History
